dialogue.utterance: Unit 2, dispatched for a seizure at 14th and Broad in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Mike, an EMT. What brought you to call 911 today?
My brother had a seizure-right here on the floor about five or six minutes ago.
Mr. Smith, do you know where you are?
Can you open your eyes for me?
What's today's date?
Can you squeeze my fingers for me?
Airway is patent and not showing signs of compromise at this time.
Pulse is 94 by palpation.
Respirations are 19, even, and uniform in depth.
Blood pressure is 101 over 50, measured on the left arm while seated.
Blood glucose is 81 mg/dL using fingerstick.
SpO₂ is 94% using pulse oximetry on room air.
EKG shows sinus rhythm with no acute changes.
Has he ever had seizures before?
Yeah, almost on a regular basis.
Are you aware of any trauma in his past?
Oh yeah. He was shot in the back of the head in 2019. His mother told me.
What were you doing before the seizure today?
He was just sitting here, smoking. I don't know how long ago, but it felt like minutes.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you describe his pain now?
Zero-he didn't look in pain at all during or after the seizure. Just confused and disoriented afterward.
Is he responding to questions or commands now?
Not really. He's just staring off like he's not aware of anything around him.
GCS is 14-eye opening 4, verbal 4, motor 6.
Skin appears normal with no visible rashes or wounds.
No signs of trauma noted on head or neck.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Partner
dialogue.utterance: No drainage or obstructing material in ears or nose.
Taking breaths regularly without apparent distress.
Abdomen not examined due to altered mental status.
No obvious deformities or injuries to the extremities; IV placed on the arm.
Let's apply the 4-lead ECG for continuous monitoring.
ECG is on-reading shows sinus rhythm with a normal interpretation at this time.
Let's proceed with starting an IV line for medication administration if needed.

dialogue.micro_intent: iv start - extremity vein (arm or leg)
dialogue.utterance: IV successfully started on the right arm-venous access achieved.
Declaring protocol 3-12: Seizure management under standing orders. Patient is stable and not seizing now.
Retaking pulse and other vitals now for reassessment.
Current pulse is 86-slightly slower than before, but regular.
Respiratory rate is 18-no signs of distress or abnormality.
Blood pressure is now 114 over 68-stable without need for intervention.
Glucose meter is not functioning-reading is zero. Possible equipment malfunction.
SpO₂ is 98%-an improvement and now within normal range on room air.
EKG remains in sinus rhythm and stable from prior assessment.
Advise is to transport to Chippenham Hospital for further evaluation and management.
That's correct. Our hospital. No issues with that.
We'll need to move him carefully due to altered mental status-let's secure the reeves sleeve now.
Reeves sleeve secured. Ready for transfer from upstairs.
Three, two, one-carry. Transfer complete. Now positioning on the stretcher.
Patient is on the stretcher, supine and strapped down. Positions secure for transport. Ready for ambulance.
All clear. Patient is now in the ambulance and stable. En route to the hospital.
RN, transferring an 18-year-old male following a seizure approximately 5-6 minutes in duration. Prior seizure history and a head trauma in 2019.
Current vitals are BP 114/68, pulse 86, SpO₂ 98%. IV in place; blood glucose reading is unreliable.
Initial ECG showed sinus rhythm. Mental status is altered according to family. GCS 14 documented in field.
All equipment and IV fluid are included. Transfer to your team now complete.
Thank you, Mike. Received the stable vitals and IV placement. Handover complete.
